what_it_does: A minimal stdlib HTTP service that wraps the Kokoro ONNX text-to-speech engine for local CPU inference. It auto-downloads model files on first run, warms the engine before serving, and exposes endpoints to synthesize WAV audio (/tts), list voices (/voices), and report health (/health).
observations: Uses only the Python stdlib http.server plus kokoro-onnx and soundfile.
Downloads kokoro-v1.0.onnx and voices-v1.0.bin from a GitHub release on first run.
Keeps a global singleton _kokoro engine loaded lazily and warmed at startup.
POST /tts accepts JSON {text, voice?, speed?} and returns audio/wav bytes.
GET /voices returns sorted voices and the default voice; falls back to DEFAULT_VOICE on error.
Default voice is configurable via COMPANY_TTS_VOICE environment variable.
Hardcodes language to en-us and uses 127.0.0.1 binding.
Suppresses default HTTP request logging via log_message no-op.
Fail-loud error handling in do_POST returns exception type and message.
standouts: Self-contained local TTS microservice with no web framework.
Automatic large-model download from a public release URL.
Global singleton engine pattern with startup warming.
conventions: Module-level constants and paths (HERE, MODELS, MODEL, VOICES, REL, DEFAULT_VOICE).
__main__ guard for CLI port argument and server startup.
Type hints on the internal _wav helper.
Environment-variable configuration with a COMPANY_ prefix.
concerns: Broad except in do_POST exposes internal exception details to clients.
No input validation beyond checking for non-empty text (no length limit, no speed range check, no content-type enforcement).
No checksum or signature verification for downloaded model files.
No request logging, authentication, rate limiting, or request-size limits.
Global mutable engine state may have unclear thread-safety under concurrent ThreadingHTTPServer requests.
Production-grade robustness of ThreadingHTTPServer is limited.
notes: A tiny, self-hosted Kokoro TTS HTTP backend that downloads its own model and serves WAV audio for a bridge to proxy.
questions: How is the external bridge configured to proxy /api/tts to this service?
What is the full set of supported voices and where is the voice registry documented?
Is the global Kokoro engine thread-safe under concurrent /tts requests?
Are there plans to support languages other than en-us?
How are model updates or corrupted downloads handled?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: a local CPU TTS HTTP service with auto-download, voice listing, and WAV synthesis endpoints.
intention_signals: Provide a lightweight, standalone TTS endpoint that can be started locally and proxied by a larger application, keeping the stack simple and avoiding GPU dependencies.
contribution: A self-contained, CPU-only TTS HTTP wrapper around Kokoro that auto-downloads its model, exposes synthesis and voice-registry endpoints, and can serve as a drop-in local voice backend for a proxied system.
summary_for_embedding: A minimal stdlib HTTP service wrapping the Kokoro ONNX text-to-speech engine for local CPU synthesis, exposing /tts and /voices endpoints and auto-downloading model files on first run.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a lightweight, VRAM-free local TTS backend that the main application bridge can proxy for speech generation.

symbols.does: Downloads Kokoro model and voice files if they are not already present locally.
symbols.description: Creates the models directory and uses urllib to fetch kokoro-v1.0.onnx and voices-v1.0.bin from a GitHub release URL when missing, blocking until complete.

symbols.does: Lazily initializes and returns the global Kokoro TTS engine.
symbols.description: Uses a module-level singleton _kokoro; on first call it ensures models exist, imports kokoro_onnx.Kokoro, and constructs the engine with the local model and voice files.

symbols.does: Synthesizes WAV audio bytes for the given text, voice, and speed.
symbols.description: Calls the Kokoro engine to create samples at the configured speed and language en-us, then writes them to an in-memory WAV buffer using soundfile and returns the bytes.

symbols.does: HTTP request handler class for the TTS service endpoints.
symbols.description: Subclass of BaseHTTPRequestHandler implementing GET /voices and /health, POST /tts, a shared response helper, and a suppressed log_message.

symbols.does: Writes an HTTP response with the given status, body, and content type.
symbols.description: Encodes string bodies to bytes, sends the response code, Content-Type, Content-Length headers, and writes the body to the client.

symbols.does: Handles GET requests for /voices and health endpoints.
symbols.description: Returns a JSON list of available voices plus the default voice, or a simple ok response for / and /health; returns 404 for unknown paths.

symbols.does: Handles POST /tts by synthesizing speech from JSON input.
symbols.description: Parses JSON, validates that text is non-empty, calls _wav with the requested or default voice and speed, and returns audio/wav; catches exceptions and returns a 500 JSON error with details.
Overrides BaseHTTPRequestHandler.log_message to do nothing, preventing per-request log lines.
